entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): New Testament: Grade 12, Church History: Grades 10-12, Spiritual Formation: Grade 12

About Old Testament: Grades 10-12
Read the OT chronologically and study NT epistles. Students use commentaries/guides to understand cultural, historical, and theological contexts. Students also read books on particular topics to help them grow spiritually and study Church History connected to the time period., Read the OT chronologically and study NT epistles.  Grade 12 also studies Psalms. Students use commentaries/guides to understand cultural, historical, and theological contexts. Students also read books on particular topics to help them grow spiritually and study Church History connected to the time period.

About Church History: Grades 10-12
Using a survey of key events in the American church, articles, and extra resources on Christianity across the world in the lesson plans, this course gives students a personal and big picture look at church history during the 19th century. A second text is used to bring the people and events of the early church to life.

About New Testament: Grade 12
Each year in high school, students read one of the gospels along with several other New Testament epistles. For 2026-2027 they read the gospel of Luke along with Romans, Ephesians, Hebrews, and Revelation. Using their study Bibles and other resources, students encounter scripture and seek to understand the cultural, historical, and theological context of their readings in order to more deeply appreciate the richness of the Bible and the humility needed in order to live out God's word today.

About Spiritual Formation: Grade 12
Spiritual formation courses cover particular topics in the Christian life to encourage and deepen students' faith. This course helps students use their imaginations to be changed by God, reflect on what it means to be an embodied soul made in God's image, and deepen their delight in God.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
New Testament: Grade 12
Lessons in one of the gospels are shared across HS grades. Some epistles studied in Grade 12 are more challenging than those in younger grades, but lessons can be combined if desired. Students new to reading the Bible for themselves or unused to commentaries or study notes may find the lessons in Grades 7-8 a gentler introduction to these study practices.

Spiritual Formation: Grade 12
Appropriate for interested students in Grades 10-11.

blocks.text: Take a moment to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

Read the passage assigned or read for the number of minutes allocated. If a book is challenging for you, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently now and then to make sure you are tracking. Or you could listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along. You may also want to read aloud to yourself. If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your Commonplace Book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. You might want to set a timer to keep yourself on track, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

Read notes in your Study Bible or commentary. Look up places mentioned in the text in your Bible atlas. Add something to your Book of Centuries or Commonplace Book. Jot down additional reflections and thoughts in your Bible notebook or share with others.

Read over the lesson plans to familiarize yourself with the content for accountability and interest. If doing the lessons together, use a picture, a map, or an engaging question to rouse students' interest before reading. You may also highlight one or two unfamiliar terms if needed. If in a classroom context, you may choose to leave larger doctrinal conversations for students to have with their parents at home.

lessons.title: 30m Old Testament: Grades 7-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Old Testament Today, notebook
➜ INTRO (For Grades 7-12)
This year you will be reading 2 Samuel, I Kings, I & 2 Chronicles, selections of Psalms, Proverbs and Ecclesiastes. You will be reading the passages chronologically or in the order of the events. "The goal of studying the Bible chronologically is not to replace the time-honored canonical arrangement, but instead to honor time as a setting in which the biblical record appeared. Readers who study the Bible this way will return to their traditional Bibles better equipped to read them. Acquiring a sense of the flow of the Bible's story will put flesh and blood on its message" (from Chronological Guide to the Bible).
1. Your ESV Concise Study Bible (Grades 7-8) or Cultural Backgrounds 
Study Bible (Grade 9-12) will have maps, articles, and notes to help you
along the way. If you have a question about a specific verse, see if there is
a note about that verse below the text.
2. Use your Student Bible Atlas or atlas in your Bible to locate places as
needed.
3. We will also utilize Old Testament Today and the Bible Project website 
as a handbook for helping us understand the background information we 
need in order to be informed readers.

➜ VIEW
1. Throughout the year, you will watch short videos of Dr. John Walton, the
co-author of OT Today. The videos were created specifically for Alveary
students. We hope they help you understand the Bible better. 
2. You will be reading selections from the Psalms throughout the year. Watch this video to learn more about the Psalms. 
∞ Video Link: Psalms

➜ READ, NARRATE & RESPOND
The psalms are difficult to tie to a specific historical context. They are the worship songs of Israel and are intended to apply to the needs of all worshippers across time. Some psalms do have historical superscriptions that make it possible to associate them with specific events, but these make up only about 10% of all the psalms. Another criterion is to place the psalms ascribed to David (or Solomon or Moses) during the life of that person. A second criterion is to place pslams according to their type (from Chronological Guide to the Bible)>
Psalms 79-82

lessons.title: 30m New Testament: Grade 12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
➜ INTRO
Each year, we will be reading one of the Gospels along with our study of the rest of the New Testament books. This year, we will be reading Mark along with Romans, Ephesians, Hebrews, and Revelation. It is helpful to be continually reminded of who Jesus is and how he interacted with people. The Gospel accounts (and all the rest!) were designed to be read many times. There will be insights gained that you would only pick up after multiple readings and by paying attention to repeated words and themes. 

We will make use of the Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible notes, the Chronological Guide, and some Bible Project videos/articles/podcasts to help us understand what Mark and Paul are saying to their audiences.  Then we can think about what new thoughts about God we gain in the process.  You do not need to read all the study notes for all the verses.  Pick and choose based on which verses you have questions about. Use a digital or physical notebook to record your thoughts as you work your way through the readings. 

➜ VIEW & NARRATE
Today, we will set the stage for reading Mark by gaining some context. Narrate silently in your head or by talking to yourself to go over the main points. Or, jot them down in your journal.

∞ Video Link: How to Read the New Testament
∞ Video Link: How to Read the Gospel
∞ Video Link: Mark Summary
 
➜ PRINT
You may want to print out the summary of the book to reference as you read.
∞ Image Link: Mark

➜ MAP WORK
When places are named in the text refer to a map in the study Bible. Finding the places on the map is very helpful for seeing the stories in the context of the land. Sketching your own maps of journeys or regions can be helpful too.

lessons.title: 30m Spiritual Formation: Grade 12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Introduction
⍞ Materials: Becoming by Beholding

➜ INTRO
How would you define Imagination?  What role do you think it plays in your spiritual formation or growth? What do you think the title of the book means or is referring to? You may find it helpful to annotate while you read. Sometimes for a first reading it can be helpful to wait to read the footnotes until you finish reading the assigned pages. Then go back and read the footnote for sentences in the reading that interested you at a later time. 

➜ READ
Introduction

➜ NARRATE
Capture the main points of the Introduction in writing. What is the plan of the book? Make a list of 5-7 things you behold regularly. Write one word for what each one might be forming in you.

➜ DISCUSS (in a group, with your teacher, or in writing on your own if you have time)
• Discuss the quote from page x, "Looking through the eyes of Christians from the past is like trying on spectacles that remedy our myopic fixation on the present and give us a more vivid view of the fullness of the gospel."  How does this work? Why are we fixated on the present?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE

lessons.body:
What is Creation Part 1
⍞ Materials: Becoming by Beholding

➜ INTRO
If nature study is a regular part of your Charlotte Mason relational education, many of this chapter's ideas will be very familiar to you.  If you are new to nature study, today's reading will give you some of the deeper reasons why nature study is so important.  Bestiaries will probably be new for everyone!

➜ READ
Ch.2 p.27-42 "A lion marches" - "to a wider audience."  

➜ NARRATE
Silently to yourself go over Davis's main points or make a bullet point list.  Write briefly about one that stood out to you.

➜ DISCUSS (in a group, with your teacher, or in writing on your own if you have time)
• Discuss the quote from page 30, " Focusing on human set-apartness at the expense of our interconnectedness with the natural world impoverishes our view not only of the natural world but also of ourselves and of God."

lessons.title: 30m Spiritual Formation: Grade 12 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
How Should We Read Scripture Part 1
⍞ Materials: Becoming by Beholding

➜ INTRO
As this book is a bit longer than what can readily fit in 11 weeks, please read Chapter 3 at another time if you can.  It is a fascinating look at how church architecture forms us. There are delightful images from Chartres Cathedral to consider. For today we will begin thinking about how we read Scripture. Write down a few words that would describe your current practices.

➜ READ
p.57-58, Ch.4 p.81-96 "How heavy" - "into the Word."  

➜ NARRATE
Describe the parts of lectio divina and what the purpose is. Describe Ignatian imaginative prayer. How do we engage our five senses in the process?

➜ DISCUSS (in a group, with your teacher, or in writing on your own if you have time)
• Discuss the quote from page 94, "It is all too easy to make Jesus who we want him to be, but praying with Scripture roots us in the Jesus of the Gospels." How?

lessons.title: 30m Spiritual Formation: Grade 12 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Being God's Image: Why Creation Still Matters

➜ INTRO
As you work your way through this fascinating study on the implications of us being God's image, there are some extra resources at the back of the book. Starting on p.189, there are QR codes arranged by chapter that take you to short videos by the Bible Project that discuss elements of the chapter that you may find helpful. Also, starting on p.195, there are discussion questions that may be helpful to spur your thinking and reflecting as you narrate after reading. Make use of both resources if they are helpful to you. 

➜ READ
Foreword, Introduction

➜ NARRATE
Find a way that works well for you to interact with the ideas in each week's reading. You might want to read and narrate silently to yourself and then discuss the chapter with someone. Or, you might want to write out what the chapter was about as you think through the ideas. Maybe you want to draw a picture or a diagram that helps you picture what was being discussed. Different chapters might lend themselves to different ways of narrating. FInd what works well for you, or try something new to see how that might be helpful.

Read the following passage and explain what the author is communicating:
"What they passed on was not primarily a book, or a set of truths. It was a life. It was the truth...There are some things that you can only learn by absorbing them. terms.content:
Church History: Grades 10-12
· When the Church Was Young: Select an Early Church Father from the reading that you enjoyed learning about and identify several things you learned from him. Explain how you are incorporating those things into your daily living.

New Testament: Grade 12
· • NT: What was Mark's emphasis in his gospel? What new thoughts did this reading provide about who Jesus is? What other insights did you gain?

Spiritual Formation: Grade 12
· After reading the Conclusion of the book, write what has changed in how you see? What do you want to keep beholding? What exercise formed you most this term?

Church History: Grades 10-12
· When the Church Was Young: Write about the impact persecution had on the early church. How did the Early Church Fathers respond to the persecution they faced? Include examples.

New Testament: Grade 12
· • NT: What is the overall structure and message of Romans? What new thoughts did this reading provide? What other insights did you gain?

· • NT: Tell the connection between unity and diversity of gifts, OR Tell the evidence of spiritual growth Paul mentions in Ephesians. What new thoughts did this reading provide? What other insights did you gain?

Spiritual Formation: Grade 12
· Being God's Image: Why Creation Still Matters: What does it mean to be God's Image and why does creation still matter? What effect does this have on your identity and purpose?

Church History: Grades 10-12
· When the Church Was Young: Write about three important takeaways from the reading that have impacted your daily life and explain why.

New Testament: Grade 12
· • NT: Discuss Jesus, High Priest of a Better Covenant, in Hebrews 8 and relate it to your OT readings. OR Tell what you know of the authorship and aim of the letter to the Hebrews. At what points does the author show the supremacy of Christ? (You can use your Bible to give quotations.) What new thoughts did this reading provide? What other insights did you gain?

· • NT: What is the structure of Revelation, and what is the main message? What new thoughts did this reading provide? What other insights did you gain?

Spiritual Formation: Grade 12
· Delighting in the Trinity: Why is delighting in the Trinity so important? Why does it matter? What changes has it brought about in you?
